Late Fee Calculator Compliance

Understanding GST Late Fees and 18% Interest Rules

Missed your GST filing deadline? Learn how the government calculates late fees and 18% interest on delayed GSTR-3B and GSTR-1 returns.

18 June 2026

Filing your GST returns on time is the bedrock of compliance in India. Missing a deadline doesn’t just trigger automated notices from the tax department; it also results in the compounding accumulation of late fees and interest.

If you’ve missed a due date on your GST Calendar, it’s important to understand exactly how much you owe. You can calculate your liability instantly using our free Late Fee Calculator.

How GST Late Fees are Calculated

Late fees are calculated on a per-day basis for every day the return is delayed past the statutory due date. The fee is split equally between Central GST (CGST) and State GST (SGST).

1. For Normal Returns (Tax Liability Exists)

If you have outward supplies and a tax liability for the month:

  • Fee per day: ₹50 (₹25 CGST + ₹25 SGST)
  • Maximum Capping: The maximum late fee is capped based on your Annual Aggregate Turnover (AATO). For businesses with a turnover up to ₹1.5 crore, the cap is usually ₹2,000 (₹1,000 CGST + ₹1,000 SGST). For larger businesses, it can go up to ₹10,000.

2. For NIL Returns (No Tax Liability)

If you have zero sales and zero purchases for the month, you must still file a “NIL” return. However, the late fee penalty is reduced:

  • Fee per day: ₹20 (₹10 CGST + ₹10 SGST)
  • Maximum Capping: The maximum late fee for a NIL return is capped at ₹500 (₹250 CGST + ₹250 SGST).

The 18% Interest Rule on Delayed Payments

Late fees are only half the penalty. If you delay your tax payment, the government charges interest under Section 50 of the CGST Act.

  • Interest Rate: 18% per annum.
  • How it is applied: Interest is calculated only on the net tax liability paid in cash. It is not calculated on the portion of the tax paid using your Input Tax Credit (ITC) ledger.
  • Calculation Formula: (Net Tax Liability × 18% × Number of Days Delayed) / 365

Example

You had a tax liability of ₹10,000 to be paid in cash. You filed your GSTR-3B 10 days late.

  • Interest = (10,000 × 18 × 10) / (100 × 365) = ₹49.31
  • Late Fee = 10 days × ₹50 = ₹500
  • Total Penalty = ₹549.31

Avoiding Penalties

The simplest way to avoid these penalties is to track your due dates rigorously. Bookmark our Due Dates Calendar and set internal reminders. If you are ever in doubt about the exact tax calculation to report, use our GST Calculator to ensure your numbers match your invoice.

Remember, the official GST Portal auto-populates late fees in your next month’s GSTR-3B. It is always better to pay it promptly to avoid further compounding!
